text: The surgeon begins dissection of the gastrocolic ligament approximately six centimeters from the pylorus using a harmonic scalpel. The dissection continues along the major curvature of the stomach in a cephalad direction, revealing the pancreas. Pancreatic dissection starts proximal to the cystic mass at the superior border of the pancreas, with careful attention to hemostasis. A hook electrocautery is used alongside the harmonic scalpel for precise tissue dissection. The splenic artery is identified and carefully isolated using a Maryland dissector and hook electrocautery.

A red vessel loop encircles the splenic artery for proximal control, secured with a 5mm endo clip. Dissection continues along the pancreas's inferior border using a harmonic scalpel, while a hook electrocautery divides posterior connective tissue attachments. The pancreas is elevated anteriorly, and a Maryland dissector is inserted behind it near the portal vein confluence. A medium-sized Penrose drain encircles and elevates the pancreas in preparation for transection.

The surgeon inserts a laparoscopic linear stapler with tissue reinforcement material, elevating the pancreas using a Penrose drain for transection. The pancreas is transected with good hemostasis at the cut edges. Dissection along the tortuous splenic artery and vein is performed using a hook electrocautery and harmonic scalpel, with a red vessel loop providing traction during the dissection of the splenic artery from the pancreas parenchyma. The dissection continues toward the splenic hilum until the specimen is completely detached.
